content.full_text:
SUPERSEDED
by: 1842/95-365

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

February 25, 1992


Honorable Diane E. Watson
California State Senate, Room 4040
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Your Request for Confirmation of
Telephone Advice
Our File Number I-92-62

Dear Senator Watson:

This is in response to your request for assistance relative
to the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act¹ (the
"Act"). Since your advice request is general in nature, we are
treating your letter as a request for informal assistance.² 
Furthermore, this advice is limited to provisions of the Act. You
should consult your legislative counsel to determine the effect of
other laws on your proposed action, such as Penal Code Section
424.

QUESTIONS

1. May you use campaign funds to install and operate in your
capitol office a private phone which is to be used for political
purposes?

2. May you use your capitol staff to answer the private campaign
phone and take messages on your behalf?


___________________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. I-92-62
Page 2


CONCLUSIONS

1. Campaign funds may be used to install and operate a telephone
in your Capitol office for use in your campaign. It is an
expenditure to seek office which is reasonably related to a
political purpose.

2. The Act prohibits the use of public money, including staff
services, for purposes of seeking elective office. However, the
limited use of staff services for activities such as answering the
campaign telephone or taking messages on your behalf would not
violate the Act if your Capitol staff spends less than 10 percent
of its time engaged in campaign-related activities.

FACTS

You are presently a member of the California State Senate.
You are also a candidate for the Los Angeles Board of Supervisors.
You wish to make expenditures from your campaign funds for
expenses in connection with the installation and operation of a
telephone in your capitol office which will be used in your
campaign. You do not anticipate that any staff time will be used
to answer the campaign phone or to take messages.

ANALYSIS

Campaign funds may be used for an expenditure to seek office
so long as you can articulate a reasonable relation between the
expenditure of funds and a political purpose. Expenditures which
confer a substantial personal benefit must be directly related to
a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section
89512.) Based on your facts, the expenditure of funds for the
installation and operation of a telephone for the campaign is
permissible.³

Your letter states the campaign phone will be installed in
your capitol office. The use of public money for purposes of
seeking elective office is a violation of Section 85300 of the
Act. Section 85300, added to the Act in June of 1988 by
Proposition 73, provides:

No public officer shall expend and no
candidate shall accept any public moneys for the
purpose of seeking elective office.

___________________
3 Section 89517 provides that an expenditure of campaign funds
for the purchase, lease, or refurbishment of any appliance or
equipment is considered to be directly related to a political,
legislative or governmental purpose as long as its use for other
purposes is only incidental to its use for political, legislative,
or governmental purposes. You did not provide any facts in your
letter to determine whether this section of the Act is applicable.

Our File No. I-92-62
Page 3


"Public moneys" is defined to include all bonds and evidence
of indebtedness, and all moneys belonging to the state, or any
city, county, town, district, or public agency therein, and all
moneys, bonds, and evidence of indebtedness received or held by
state, county, district, city, town, or public agency officers in
their official capacity. (Section 85102(e); Penal Code Section
426.) While this provision specifically refers only to the use of
public money, it includes by implication all monetary costs as-
sociated with the shift of staff services as well. (People v. Sperl
(1976) 54 Cal.App.3d 640, 657.) Section 85300, therefore,
prohibits the use of public moneys for the purpose of seeking
elective office and any use of public resources for which charges
are incurred and paid with public money.

Section 85300 creates an absolute prohibition on the
intentional use of public money for campaign related activity.
However, the Act recognizes that the campaign services of an
employee to a candidate are not contributory unless the employee
spends over 10 percent of his or her compensated time in any one
month on campaign activities. (Regulation 18423; Gallo Advice
Letter, No. I-90-234.) Thus, such limited use of your staff for
activities such as answering the phone or taking messages on your
behalf does not violate Section 85300 of the Act if your Capitol
staff spends less than 10 percent of its time engaged in campaign-
related activities.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

[signature: Luisa Menchaca]

By: Luisa Menchaca
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
1. May you use campaign funds to install and operate in your capitol office a private phone which is to be used for political purposes?

2. May you use your capitol staff to answer the private campaign phone and take messages on your behalf?

---

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)
sections.conclusion:
1. Campaign funds may be used to install and operate a telephone in your Capitol office for use in your campaign. It is an expenditure to seek office which is reasonably related to a political purpose.

2. The Act prohibits the use of public money, including staff services, for purposes of seeking elective office. However, the limited use of staff services for activities such as answering the campaign telephone or taking messages on your behalf would not violate the Act if your Capitol staff spends less than 10 percent of its time engaged in campaign-related activities.
sections.facts: You are presently a member of the California State Senate. You are also a candidate for the Los Angeles Board of Supervisors. You wish to make expenditures from your campaign funds for expenses in connection with the installation and operation of a telephone in your capitol office which will be used in your campaign. You do not anticipate that any staff time will be used to answer the campaign phone or to take messages.
sections.analysis:
Campaign funds may be used for an expenditure to seek office so long as you can articulate a reasonable relation between the expenditure of funds and a political purpose. Expenditures which confer a substantial personal benefit must be directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89512.) Based on your facts, the expenditure of funds for the installation and operation of a telephone for the campaign is permissible.3

Your letter states the campaign phone will be installed in your capitol office. The use of public money for purposes of seeking elective office is a violation of Section 85300 of the Act. Section 85300, added to the Act in June of 1988 by Proposition 73, provides:

No public officer shall expend and no candidate shall accept any public moneys for the purpose of seeking elective office.

3 Section 89517 provides that an expenditure of campaign funds for the purchase, lease, or refurbishment of any appliance or equipment is considered to be directly related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose as long as its use for other purposes is only incidental to its use for political, legislative, or governmental purposes. You did not provide any facts in your letter to determine whether this section of the Act is applicable.

"Public moneys" is defined to include all bonds and evidence of indebtedness, and all moneys belonging to the state, or any city, county, town, district, or public agency therein, and all moneys, bonds, and evidence of indebtedness received or held by state, county, district, city, town, or public agency officers in their official capacity. (Section 85102(e); Penal Code Section 426.) While this provision specifically refers only to the use of public money, it includes by implication all monetary costs associated with the use of staff services as well. (People v. Sperl (1976) 54 Cal.App.3d 640, 657.) Section 85300, therefore, prohibits the use of public moneys for the purpose of seeking elective office and any use of public resources for which charges are incurred and paid with public money.

Section 85300 creates an absolute prohibition on the intentional use of public money for campaign related activity. However, the Act recognizes that the campaign services of an employee to a candidate are not contributions unless the employee spends over 10 percent of his or her compensated time in any one month on campaign activities. (Regulation 18423; Gallo Advice Letter, No. I-90-234.) Thus, such limited use of your staff for activities such as answering the phone or taking messages on your behalf does not violate Section 85300 of the Act if your Capitol staff spends less than 10 percent of its time engaged in campaign-related activities.
